title: Chicken tikka
subtitle: Marinated chicken pieces grilled until charred
excerpt: Boneless chicken pieces marinated in spiced yoghurt and char-grilled until the surface is deeply coloured and the spice coating has caramelised. The tikka pieces are the starting point for chicken tikka masala, but are complete as a starter or side in their own right, smoky, juicy, with the characteristic orange-red marinade crust.
sourceNotes: Tikka (meaning 'piece' or 'chunk' in Turkish/Urdu) refers to the cut and cooking method. In traditional Punjabi cooking, tikka is cooked in a tandoor clay oven. The domestic version uses a very hot grill or griddle pan to approximate the charring. Chicken tikka has been on British Indian menus since the 1970s.

content.text: The marinade does two jobs: it flavours the chicken and the yoghurt tenderises the surface. The spices must be in direct contact with the chicken for the flavour to penetrate, cut the thigh pieces so they are about 4 cm across and make a shallow cut in each piece to help the marinade penetrate the centre. A 2-hour marinade is adequate; overnight is better.

The defining quality of tikka is the char. Without a tandoor the best domestic approximation is a cast-iron griddle pan preheated until smoking, or a very hot grill positioned close to the heat source. The goal is surface colouration (dark patches where the marinade has caramelised) while the chicken remains moist inside. This requires high heat and a short cook, not medium heat and a long one.

Put the {{plain-yoghurt}} in a bowl with the {{garlic}} and {{ginger-root}}. Add the {{chilli-powder}}, {{garam-masala}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{turmeric}}. Stir in the lemon juice, {{vegetable-oil}}, and salt to make a thick marinade. Add the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} and turn to coat. Cover and chill for at least 2 hours.

Preheat a cast-iron griddle pan over high heat until smoking, or set the oven grill to its highest setting with the rack positioned at the top. Thread the chicken pieces onto skewers if using. Grill the chicken for 5 to 6 minutes per side, or until charred in patches and cooked through. The internal temperature should reach 74°C. Serve immediately with lemon wedges, sliced onion, and raita.

items.fix: Get the griddle pan properly smoking before the first piece goes on. Leave space between pieces, crowding drops the temperature and causes steaming rather than searing
items.cause: Pan or grill not hot enough, or too many pieces crowded together
items.symptom: No char, just grey and steamed-looking

items.fix: Wipe off excess marinade before grilling. The thin coating chars correctly; a thick layer burns before the heat reaches the centre
items.cause: Yoghurt marinade sugars burn quickly over excessive heat
items.symptom: Marinade burning before chicken cooks through

items.fix: Thigh meat stays juicier under high heat. Pull when internal temperature reaches 74°C, cooked past this, thighs still remain more forgiving than breast

The restaurant orange colour comes partly from food colouring (optional) and partly from Kashmiri chilli powder, which gives deep red colour with moderate heat. Substitute Kashmiri chilli for standard chilli powder for more colour
items.cause: No food colouring (acceptable) or insufficient chilli powder
items.symptom: Colour pale yellow rather than the orange-red of restaurant tikka

content.text: Chicken tikka does three jobs at once: starter, side, and the raw material for chicken tikka masala. On the curry-house menu it shows up in all three roles. As a starter it comes with salad, onion rings, and mint chutney. As an ingredient it goes straight into the masala sauce. The marinade (yoghurt, garlic, ginger, garam masala) is the base of the Punjabi grilled-meat tradition. It runs with little change through tandoori chicken, seekh kebabs, and paneer tikka.
